scenes.text:
Behind the abandoned theatre, the city goes strange and quiet.
The lamps are still lit here. The shutters are still painted. A cat sleeps on a windowsill like nothing has happened, because here — for now — nothing has.
A boy of seven or eight sits on the curb across from a bookshop, swinging his legs. He looks up at you and his eyes are the wrong eyes. Too still. Too — listening.
"Hello," he says, polite. "Are you the one walking to the Gate? He said someone might be along. He said to be kind. He said you have a sad face."
He smiles. The smile is his. The listening behind it isn't.
"My name is Pip," he adds. "I think it's Pip. It's harder to remember some days."

choices.text: Crouch. Talk to him like a child.
Back away. He is not safe.

scenes.text:
You crouch. The cobbles are cold through your knees.
"Pip," you say. "How long has it been hard to remember your name?"
He thinks. He thinks too long. "Since the man with the kind voice started visiting. In dreams. He tells me stories. He says I'm clever. Nobody calls me clever, miss — sir." His face flickers. "It's nice. Is it bad?"
You take his hands. They are very small and very cold and very alive.
"It's bad, Pip."
"Oh." He thinks about this. "Will you make him stop?"
The listening behind his eyes goes sharp at that. Something doesn't like the question.

do: journal:I used the wardstone on a boy named Pip. The Keeper's listening went out of him like a held breath. He cried. So did I, a little.
journal:I promised a child I'd save him. I have promised too many people too many things. I will keep this one or die trying.

scenes.text:
You press the wardstone to his forehead. It heats and then it cools.
Pip blinks. His eyes — his real, blurry, ordinary-child eyes — come back into them. He looks at you like he's never seen you before and is delighted anyway.
"Oh," he says. "My head's quiet."
He starts to cry — the easy, full crying of a child who has been holding something heavy for a long time without knowing it was heavy.
A woman runs out of the bakery across the way. "Pip — oh, Pip, baby —" She gathers him up. She looks at you over his hair, and you don't know what your face is doing but hers softens.
"Thank you," she mouths. She doesn't know for what. She knows it's something.

You promise. Pip nods solemnly, as if witnessing a contract.
"Okay," he says. He stands, dusts his pants, and walks away down the lane like a small person on an errand.
The listening doesn't leave him. It goes with him.
You feel a great weight settle, the kind that doesn't lift until you have done a thing. The bookshop door is just there.

You walk fast and you do not look back and the curb noises follow you for a block — small swinging feet, a child's hum — until you turn a corner and the street is just a street again.
You stop. You lean against a wall. Your hands are shaking.
You tell yourself you couldn't have helped. You tell yourself it isn't your job. You tell yourself a lot of things.
None of them feel true.

do: journal:I left a boy on a curb. I will see him in dreams forever, I think.

scenes.text:
The bookshop smells the way bookshops always smell — old paper, dust, a little smoke. The bell over the door is so soft you almost don't hear it.
The bookseller is a woman with one milky eye and one sharp one. She doesn't look up.
"You want the back room," she says. "Everyone who walks like you does."
She leads you past shelves that go up further than the building should allow. The back room is smaller than it should be. There is one chair. One book on a stand. The book is open.
"He had a name," she says, "before he was the Keeper. Names are doors. If you say the right one in the right place, the door doesn't have to be on his side."

choices.text: Ask if she has anything else for you.

scenes.text:
She smiles, finally. It is not a friendly smile but it is not cruel.
"A memory," she says. "Not a big one. Something small. A meal you liked. A face from a holiday. The first word someone you loved said wrong. Anything. The book takes what it likes."
She taps the open page. "Names like his are heavy. The book has to be paid."
You could lie. You could try. You suspect the book would know.

do: journal:I gave the book a memory of my mother humming in the kitchen. I cannot get it back. I remember that she hummed; I cannot remember the song. I traded it for a name.

scenes.text:
She turns the book. The page is blank — and then it isn't. A word writes itself in your handwriting, which is impossible, which is happening.
It is not a long name. It is the wrong shape for a mouth. You read it once. You feel it settle into the back of your tongue like a tooth.
"Say it at the Gate," she says. "Not before. Not after. Once. Say it once. He has to hear it from someone who knew him when. The book is making you that person, briefly."
She closes the book. She looks tired now, like she's done a lifting.
"Go," she says. "And — if you live — come back and tell me how it went. I like to know."

do: journal:I carry the Keeper's old name on the back of my tongue. It tastes like cold iron and an apology.

scenes.text:
You walk out. The bell over the door is even quieter going the other way.
The street is just a street. The cat on the windowsill opens one eye and closes it.
You will think about that name you didn't buy for a long time. Probably forever. Probably as long as the rest of forever lasts.
